preFix.title: Dyeing with tansy
preFix.subtitle: Bright yellow from Tanacetum vulgare flowers on alum-mordanted wool
preFix.excerpt: Tansy (Tanacetum vulgare) produces a strong, clean yellow on alum-mordanted wool and is one of the more reliable yellow dye plants available in the UK. The flowers are simmered to extract the dye, and an iron modifier can shift the colour toward olive-green. Handle with gloves: tansy contains thujone and must never be ingested.
preFix.sourceNotes:
- Jenny Dean, Wild Color (revised ed.): tansy as yellow dye source, thujone safety note
- Dominique Cardon, Natural Dyes: Tanacetum vulgare dye chemistry and mordant response
- General natural-dyeing practice: iron modifier, wastewater guidance for alum baths

content.text: Tansy is one of the most dependable yellow dye plants in temperate gardens and roadsides. The bright button flowers of
Tanacetum vulgare
yield a strong, warm yellow on
alum
-mordanted wool that holds up well to light and washing. An iron
modifier
can shift the result toward a soft olive-green. Gather the flowers when fully open, in mid- to late summer, and work fresh or dry them for later use.
Wear gloves when handling tansy: it contains
thujone
, a toxic compound. Never use the dye pot for food. Wash hands after contact.
Method
Step 1: Pre-mordant the wool. Dissolve alum (15% weight of fibre) in warm water in the
dye bath
vessel. Add pre-wetted wool. Raise to 80°C over 30 minutes and hold for 45 minutes. Allow to cool slightly before lifting the wool out.

Step 2: Prepare the dye bath. Place fresh or dried tansy flowers (100% weight of fibre for a strong colour) in the dye pot with enough water to allow the wool to move freely. Bring to a simmer and hold at 80°C for 45 minutes.
Step 3: Strain the bath. Pour the hot dye liquor through a muslin straining bag into a clean stainless vessel, removing all plant material.

Step 4: Dye the wool. Add the pre-mordanted wool to the strained dye bath. Raise slowly to 75-80°C and hold for 1 hour, turning the wool gently every 10-15 minutes to ensure even colour. The bath should visibly
exhaust
to a pale yellow.

Step 5: Cool and rinse. Remove the pot from heat and allow wool to cool in the bath for 20-30 minutes. Lift out and rinse in water of a similar temperature to avoid shocking the fibres. Rinse until water runs clear.
Step 6: Dry. Squeeze gently (do not wring) and dry flat or hung away from direct sunlight.
Variations

Iron modifier: After dyeing, prepare a weak iron solution (2% iron sulphate dissolved in a small amount of warm water). Add the dyed wool and hold at 60°C for 10-15 minutes. The colour will shift from bright yellow toward a muted olive-green. Rinse well after the modifier bath.

Copper modifier: A copper sulphate afterbath (2% weight of fibre) shifts the yellow toward a deeper, slightly greened gold. Use copper with the same precautions as iron: wear gloves and do not dispose of copper-containing liquor down a surface water drain.
Dried vs fresh flowers: Dried tansy gives a slightly less vivid result. Compensate by increasing the ratio to 150% weight of fibre when using dried material.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Colour is pale or washed out

content.text: Check that the mordanting step reached 80°C for the full 45 minutes. Undermordanted wool takes much less dye. Also confirm the flower ratio: less than 80% weight of fibre gives noticeably weaker results.
The dye bath was too hot or the wool was moved too infrequently. Tansy exhausts quickly; turn the wool every 10 minutes and keep the temperature steady below 82°C to avoid felting and uneven strike.

Either the mordant was insufficient or the rinse water was too hot, stripping some dye. Rewet in a weak alum solution and allow to dry; this can partially restore depth. Wash dyed wool in cool water with pH-neutral soap.
Wastewater disposal

A tansy dye bath used with alum mordant only can be disposed of down a foul drain (the drain connected to the sewage system, not surface water) or diluted and added to compost in small home quantities. If an iron or copper modifier was used, dispose of that modifier bath via a foul drain only; do not add metallic modifier liquors to the garden or compost. Allow all liquors to cool before pouring.
